does: This paper introduces PEBS, a per-rater empirical-Bayes shrinkage method for calibrating reward models in RLHF systems. The core contribution is developing a closed-form post-hoc estimator that fits individual affine calibrators (scale and offset adjustments) for each annotator rather than using a single global calibrator. PEBS applies Morris-James-Stein empirical-Bayes shrinkage to regularize per-rater parameters toward the population mean, requiring only a held-out slice of each annotator's ratings without retraining the underlying reward model. The method achieves 8.58% reduction in within-user held-out RMSE on PRISM ratings and 9.66% reduction on PluriHarms harm ratings, demonstrating consistent improvements in prediction accuracy for individual raters.
connects: This work builds on the long-standing problem of handling heterogeneous annotator behavior in preference learning and reward modeling. It directly responds to limitations in existing RLHF pipelines that pool diverse raters into a single global model, losing valuable information about systematic individual differences in rating scale usage. The approach combines classical empirical-Bayes shrinkage estimation—a technique developed in statistics for balancing individual-level fitting with population-level regularization—with modern reward modeling in language model alignment. It extends prior work on annotator-specific modeling and preference aggregation by providing a principled, computationally efficient alternative to retraining or complex multi-rater architectures.
enables: This method opens directions for improving reward model robustness and interpretability in large-scale RLHF systems. It enables better understanding of individual annotator biases and scale usage patterns without modifying the base reward model, making it compatible with deployed systems. The approach could support downstream applications like detecting unreliable annotators, personalizing reward models for different use cases, or improving confidence calibration in preference prediction. Future work might extend per-rater calibration to non-linear transformations, apply similar shrinkage methods to other model parameters, or combine per-rater estimation with active learning to optimize which annotators to collect additional data from. This provides a foundation for more nuanced handling of annotator heterogeneity in human feedback systems at scale.
